metadata.title: The Gift and The Curse of Staying Private

conclusion: Gurley's warning is that venture has become a different machine. Mega funds, secondaries, stale marks, and closed IPO/M&A windows let winners stay private longer, but they also trap LP capital and move compounding away from public investors.
background: Bill Gurley returns for a state-of-venture conversation in summer 2025. His frame is systems thinking: founders, mega funds, LPs, secondary markets, banks, regulators, and public buyers are all acting rationally, but their combined behavior has changed the venture cycle. The discussion centers on the rise of multi-billion-dollar venture platforms, the backlog of private unicorns created around 2021, weak IPO/M&A exits despite a strong NASDAQ, and new late-stage private markets that can look like public markets without public disclosure.
important_facts: Branded VC firms that once raised roughly $500M every three or four years may now manage commitments closer to $5B, while $300M checks can go into 12-month-old AI companies that are not really 'late stage' except by check size. The discussion centers on the rise of multi-billion-dollar venture platforms, the backlog of private unicorns created around 2021, weak IPO/M&A exits despite a strong NASDAQ, and new late-stage private markets that can look like public markets without public disclosure.

Gurley estimates roughly 900-1,250 private companies have raised at $1B-plus valuations; many raised $200-300M each, or about $300B in aggregate, with NVCA estimating around $3T of venture assets on LP books. Key quantitative evidence: Branded VC firms that once raised roughly $500M every three or four years may now manage commitments closer to $5B, while $300M checks can go into 12-month-old AI companies that are not really 'late stage' except by check size.

2024 NASDAQ was up about 30% while the IPO window stayed mostly closed. Gurley cites IPO underpricing of roughly 25-26% plus 7% fees, or about a 33% cost of capital, versus founders who can raise private money at higher prices. Gurley estimates roughly 900-1,250 private companies have raised at $1B-plus valuations; many raised $200-300M each, or about $300B in aggregate, with NVCA estimating around $3T of venture assets on LP books.

mental_model.name: Incentive Pile-Up In Private Markets
mental_model.components: The private-company backlog is not just a valuation problem; it is a governance and incentive problem. Founders anchor on peak valuation, liquidation preferences can consume much of a lower-priced sale, and many LP gatekeepers have little incentive to force markdowns. A company once marked at $2B may be worth $400M, but the cap table can make that truth painful for everyone. The IPO market is losing its role as the default growth handoff. Large private rounds and organized secondary liquidity let companies provide capital and employee liquidity without accepting IPO underpricing, seven-point fees, or quarterly public scrutiny. The best late-stage private investors can also buy 30% of a round rather than 1-2% of an IPO allocation. Public investors may be losing the most attractive compounding years. Amazon went public below $1B and public investors captured much of the trillion-dollar journey. If future Amazons remain private until hundreds of billions or even $1T, public-market savers inherit later, more mature economics.
mental_model.application: Gurley's warning is that venture has become a different machine. Mega funds, secondaries, stale marks, and closed IPO/M&A windows let winners stay private longer, but they also trap LP capital and move compounding away from public investors. Large late-stage private companies can use private rounds and secondary liquidity to delay IPOs, shifting more value capture to private holders Gurley uses SpaceX as the clearest example of a company that could compound toward trillion-dollar scale before public investors get access If founders keep avoiding 25-26% underpricing plus roughly 7% IPO fees, traditional IPO economics face pressure from private rounds, secondaries, and tokenized alternatives Branded VC firms that once raised roughly $500M every three or four years may now manage commitments closer to $5B, while $300M checks can go into 12-month-old AI companies that are not really 'late stage' except by check size. Gurley estimates roughly 900-1,250 private companies have raised at $1B-plus valuations; many raised $200-300M each, or about $300B in aggregate, with NVCA estimating around $3T of venture assets on LP books. Gurley cites IPO underpricing of roughly 25-26% plus.

key_insights.view: The private-company backlog is not just a valuation problem; it is a governance and incentive problem.
key_insights.question: Why don't stale unicorn marks correct quickly?
key_insights.answer: Founders anchor on peak valuation, liquidation preferences can consume much of a lower-priced sale, and many LP gatekeepers have little incentive to force markdowns. A company once marked at $2B may be worth $400M, but the cap table can make that truth painful for everyone. Branded VC firms that once raised roughly $500M every three or four years may now manage commitments closer to $5B, while $300M checks can go into 12-month-old AI companies that are not really 'late stage' except by check size.

key_insights.view: The IPO market is losing its role as the default growth handoff.
key_insights.question: Why stay private when public markets are strong?
key_insights.answer: Large private rounds and organized secondary liquidity let companies provide capital and employee liquidity without accepting IPO underpricing, seven-point fees, or quarterly public scrutiny. The best late-stage private investors can also buy 30% of a round rather than 1-2% of an IPO allocation. Gurley estimates roughly 900-1,250 private companies have raised at $1B-plus valuations; many raised $200-300M each, or about $300B in aggregate, with NVCA estimating around $3T of venture assets on LP books.

key_insights.view: Public investors may be losing the most attractive compounding years.
key_insights.question: What changes when great companies stay private longer?
key_insights.answer: Amazon went public below $1B and public investors captured much of the trillion-dollar journey. If future Amazons remain private until hundreds of billions or even $1T, public-market savers inherit later, more mature economics. 2024 NASDAQ was up about 30% while the IPO window stayed mostly closed. Gurley cites IPO underpricing of roughly 25-26% plus 7% fees, or about a 33% cost of capital, versus founders who can raise private money at higher prices.

top_investment_implications.thesis: Large late-stage private companies can use private rounds and secondary liquidity to delay IPOs, shifting more value capture to private holders
Gurley uses SpaceX as the clearest example of a company that could compound toward trillion-dollar scale before public investors get access
If founders keep avoiding 25-26% underpricing plus roughly 7% IPO fees, traditional IPO economics face pressure from private rounds, secondaries, and tokenized alternatives

golden_quotes: Gurley argues that in today's venture market, 'late stage' has often become a euphemism for writing a very large check.
On stale private marks, his blunt diagnosis is that almost no participant has a strong incentive to get the marks right.
His warning to public investors is that Amazon went public below a billion dollars, while future Amazons may stay private for far longer.

events.event: Last one-on-one Invest Like the Best conversation between Gurley and Patrick before this episode
Many private unicorn marks set near the COVID tech-market peak
NASDAQ rose about 30% while the IPO market remained mostly closed
US colleges and universities issued roughly $12B of debt, a sign of liquidity pressure
Harvard marketed about $1B of private assets and Yale explored a roughly $6B private-equity secondary sale
Gurley frames mega funds, stale marks, secondaries, and IPO costs as one connected system
